rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical synthesis of iodohydrins (and ethers) from alkenes using ammonium iodide as a non-toxic iodine source via an acetonitrile-stabilized iodonium intermediate.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to iodine radical and then to a CH3CN-stabilized I+ (iodonium-like) species; cathodic reduction of H+ to H2

key_intermediates.name: iodine radical (from first anodic oxidation of I-)
open carbocation (from iodonium ring-opening, invoked for norbornene rearrangement)

electron_flow: At the anode, I- is oxidized in two successive one-electron steps to give an acetonitrile-stabilized I+ species, which adds to the alkene to form an iodonium ion; Markovnikov nucleophilic attack by water (or alcohol) delivers the iodohydrin/ether, while 2H+ are reduced at the cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: molecular iodine (I2) in place of NH4I
probe_reagents.observation: Gave the iodohydrin in moderate yield (36%), lower than with NH4I, indicating in-situ I2 cannot be ruled out but is less productive than CH3CN-stabilized I+

probe_reagents.name: norborn-2-ene (radical clock / cation probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave rearranged products 39 and 40 in 64:36 ratio, consistent with a cationic (iodonium/carbocation) intermediate rather than a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: current interruption (45 min then 24 h without current)
probe_reagents.observation: No additional product formed, ruling out post-electrolysis chemistry from cathodically released metal salts

probe_reagents.name: NH4Br control
probe_reagents.observation: Formed bromohydrin; CV showed similar oxidation waves to NH4I

probe_reagents.name: NH4Cl control
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction; CV showed oxidation at higher potential, explaining non-reactivity

probe_reagents.name: styrene CV alone and with NH4I
probe_reagents.observation: No oxidation/reduction of styrene up to +1.2 V; adding NH4I gave no current increase, ruling out direct styrene oxidation / iodine-mediated styrene oxidation

notes: Authors cannot fully exclude direct reaction of in-situ generated I2 with alkene, but argue CH3CN-stabilized I+ pathway is dominant based on lower yield with I2 and CV data. Stereochemistry of product 15 and norbornene rearrangement (39/40) support an iodonium/carbocation pathway rather than a radical mechanism.
